role_summary: Fourteen-year-old visiting family in Money, Mississippi when Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam abducted, tortured, and killed him on August 28, 1955; his mother Mamie Till-Mobley insisted on an open-casket funeral at Roberts Temple Church of God in Christ in Chicago, the September 1955 photographs of which (Jet magazine, September 15, 1955) became the most-circulated images of antebellum racial violence in the modern civil rights era; his death is the precipitating event many movement participants cite for their own civil rights organizing.

photo.alt: Emmett Till, photographed by his mother on Christmas Day 1954, eight months before his murder
photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1963 without copyright renewal)

ai_reading: Till is referenced three times in the catalog (in Mississippi-organizing transcripts where his 1955 murder is the precipitating event for several interviewees' own movement entries). He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee (his murder predated the project by five decades). His standalone catalog page is added because the corpus's Mississippi cohort cannot be fully resolved without the August 1955 event their own organizing dates from; the open-casket funeral and the Jet magazine photographs are the catalog's clearest example of a non-organizer pre-movement event whose photographic legacy directly catalyzed the cohort the corpus is built around. The novel observation his page surfaces is the chronological-anchor function: Till's August 1955 murder predates the December 1955 Montgomery bus boycott by four months; for several Mississippi-born interviewees in the corpus, the Till funeral photographs reaching Chicago and traveling outward via Jet are the conscious-political-awakening moment, the catalog's primary instance of the visual-evidence-of-anti-Black-violence channel by which the next decade's organizing was recruited.
biographical_paragraph: Till was born July 25, 1941 in Chicago to Mamie Carthan Till and Louis Till; his father had been executed by the U.S. Army for rape and murder in Italy in 1945, a fact Mamie did not initially share with her son. He was raised in the Bronzeville neighborhood of Chicago and attended McCosh Elementary School. On August 20, 1955 he traveled with his cousin Curtis Jones to visit his great-uncle Moses Wright in Money, a small Tallahatchie River settlement in Leflore County, Mississippi. On August 24, 1955 Till and a group of cousins stopped at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market; what passed between Till and Carolyn Bryant inside the store was contested for sixty years (Bryant testified that he made advances and used vulgar language; Bryant publicly recanted this account in Timothy B. Tyson's 2017 The Blood of Emmett Till). On the night of August 28, 1955 Roy Bryant and his half-brother J. W. Milam abducted Till from Wright's home; they beat him, gouged out an eye, shot him, tied a 75-pound cotton-gin fan to his neck with barbed wire, and threw the body into the Tallahatchie River. His body was recovered three days later and shipped back to Chicago. Mamie Till-Mobley insisted on an open-casket funeral at Roberts Temple Church of God in Christ in Chicago, September 3-6, 1955; the photographs Jet magazine published in its September 15, 1955 issue, and the David Jackson coverage that followed, broke the national silence around antebellum racial violence in the postwar South. Bryant and Milam were acquitted by an all-white jury in September 1955; both confessed to the killing in a 1956 paid interview with William Bradford Huie in Look magazine, protected by double-jeopardy from re-prosecution. The Anti-Lynching Crusade extended through the 1990s; the federal Emmett Till Antilynching Act was signed into law in 2022. The canonical scholarly source on the case is Devery S. Anderson's 2015 University Press of Mississippi study cited below.

sources.title: Devery S. Anderson, Emmett Till: The Murder That Shocked the World and Propelled the Civil Rights Movement
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2015; WorldCat OCLC 904337998
